data.title: Canada's Government Plans Third Canadian Dollar Green Bond
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart 




  OTTAWA--The Canadian government plans to issue its third Canadian dollar-denominated "green" bond this week. 



  The finance department said Tuesday the bonds aim to meet investor demand for sustainable green investments backed by Canada's AAA credit rating. 



  The proposed offering, which remains subject to market conditions, follows the issue of a 10-year 4 billion Canadian dollars ($2.8 billion) green bond in February 2024, which was reopened in October for an additional C$2 billion. Both saw strong investor demand, with final order books of more than C$11 billion combined, the government said. 



  Canada's first green bond was a 7.5-year, C$5 billion bond in March 2022. 



  The government's green bond program seeks to advance the country's investments in renewable energy, climate action and environmental protection. Green bonds unlock private financing to speed up projects such as green infrastructure and nature conservation, the finance department said. 





  Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  February 18, 2025 12:46 ET (17:46 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Analyst brushes aside 'DOGE' jitters surrounding Iron Mountain's stock
data.description:
By Steve Gelsi

JPMorgan reiterates overweight rating and places information-management stock on positive-catalyst watch after Iron Mountain hikes its dividend

Iron Mountain Inc.'s stock has fallen 11% in the past week after one of the document-handling company's storage facilities was mentioned by Elon Musk as a purported example of government inefficiency.

The stock fell another 1.3% on Tuesday to $94.26 a share, its lowest level since last July.

JPMorgan analyst Andrew Steinerman on Tuesday said jitters are overblown after Musk's mention last week at the White House of the storage facility during an update on the work of the so-called Department of Government Efficiency, or "DOGE."

He reiterated an overweight rating on Iron Mountain (IRM) and placed the stock on positive-catalyst watch. He also cut his price target on the stock to $112 a share from $125 a share,

Musk mentioned an underground Iron Mountain data-storage mine facility in Boyers, Pa., when talking about the slow pace of government bureaucracy. Musk said the speed of the elevators in the mine mostly determines the pace of processing retirement paperwork for federal employees.

That was followed on Thursday by comments from Iron Mountain Chief Executive William Meaney, who said DOGE's work is an overall positive for the company's growth.

Iron Mountain offers cost-saving process automation and document digitization, as well as data-center updates - and said the government is a major customer. "We have been growing in both of these areas with the government over the last few years," Meaney said. "As the government continues to drive to be more efficient, we see this as a continued opportunity for the company."

JPMorgan analyst Steinerman said the fact that Iron Mountain raised its dividend shows the company's confidence in its business, including under the second presidential administration of President Trump, which has claimed wide-ranging rights through DOGE to government and taxpayer data.

Critics have said Musk and his team are misinterpreting much of what they claim to be discovering.

"In our view, the DOGE/Musk overhand should prove short-lived," Steinerman said. "DOGE is moving at a fast pace, and Iron Mountain's work has been a positive for reducing accumulated paper backlogs, by our assessment."

Instead, Wall Street may instead start to focus on the company's dividend boost, which JPMorgan's Steinerman said came sooner than anticipated.

Iron Mountain last week said it would increase its quarterly dividend to 78.5 cents a share from 71.5 cents for shareholders of record on March 17.

Including Tuesday's trades, the stock has fallen 10.2% in 2025, compared with a 4% gain by the benchmark S&P 500 SPX .

-Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-18-25 1247ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Canada Inflation Accelerates Slightly to 1.9% in January — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart 




  OTTAWA--A jump in energy costs stoked Canadian inflation at the start of the year, fueling expectations the central bank might pause its aggressive rate cutting as the country works through a period of heightened uncertainty. 



  The consumer-price index increased 0.1% in January and advanced 1.9% compared with a year earlier, Statistics Canada said Tuesday. 



  That marked the first acceleration since October, though inflation has been at or below the Bank of Canada's 2% target for six months running. 



  The pace of inflation for the latest month was in line with the consensus view of economists, and was kept in check in part by a two-month suspension of the goods-and-services tax on some purchases that was put in place in mid-December. 



  Less encouraging for central-bank policymakers was that their preferred measures of core inflation remained sticky, heating up slightly in January. 



  After the inflation report, money-market pricing for another Bank of Canada rate cut at its March policy meeting declined to about 30% from prior roughly balanced odds. With Canada's soft economic backdrop, and given lingering threats of a trade dispute between the country and the U.S., some economists argue the central bank could hit pause on rate cuts until a clearing picture of underlying price pressures emerges. 



  "There is too much underlying inflationary pressure in Canada to warrant an inflation-targeting central bank easing monetary policy further," said Derek Holt, head of capital-markets economics at Bank of Nova Scotia. "The state of the job market also doesn't merit further easing [and] GDP is hardly screaming out for further easing." 



  January's data provides the last inflation reading before the Bank of Canada's next policy decision on March 12. The central bank has forecast inflation will remain around its 2% target over the next two years, provided Canada doesn't get caught in a trade dispute with the U.S. 



  Energy prices were the biggest drivers of the acceleration in inflation last month. Prices at the pump increased 8.6% from a year earlier, and natural gas prices climbed 6% for the latest month and were 4.8% higher than in January of last year. 



  The biggest dampening effects came from the two-month pause on taxes on some sales. Prices for food saw their first yearly decrease since May 2017, thanks to a record decline in prices of food bought from restaurants, while prices for alcohol at stores and for toys, games and hobby supplies were also down sharply on a year earlier. 



  Core prices excluding volatile food and energy costs ticked higher, rising 2.2% over the last 12 months. And the trimmed mean and weighted median measures of underlying inflation preferred by the Bank of Canada also heated up, averaging 2.7% annually compared with 2.55% in December. 



  "Underlying inflation suggests that inflationary pressures are sticky and prices have increased faster in recent months than the Bank of Canada would want to see," Charles St-Arnaud, chief economist at credit-union advocacy group Alberta Central. 



  St-Arnaud said stronger underlying inflationary pressures, coupled with a relatively firm labor market in recent months, suggest the central bank governing council will opt for a pause at the March meeting to better assess the situation. 



  The Bank of Canada has been the most aggressive among the Group of Seven central banks in cutting rates that had been driven sharply higher to tackle inflation, with the bank lowering its policy rate at each of its last six meetings. The bank in its latest decision in January removed any guidance for the future direction of rates given increased uncertainty. 



  The Bank of Canada has projected gross domestic product will strengthen in 2025, though the expansion will likely be moderated by slower population growth due to reduced federal immigration targets. The labor market remains soft, with the unemployment rate at 6.6% in January, though jobs growth has strengthened in recent months after lagging labor-force expansion for more than a year. 



  President Trump's threat of tariffs on imports from its northern neighbor has cast a cloud over the outlook for Canada's economy. Ottawa has pledged to retaliate against any levies, and the Bank of Canada has cautioned a protracted trade conflict would most likely lead to weaker gross domestic product and higher prices for Canadians. 



  A 25% levy on all non-energy exports and a 10% levy on U.S.-bound energy products from Canada were set to be implemented by the White House on Feb. 4, but Trump paused the tariffs until early March. 



  Bank of Montreal chief economist Douglas Porter continues to lean toward a Bank of Canada pause next month, with headline inflation expected to pick up with the end of the tax holiday. "Although developments on the tariff front may yet have a big say in that call," he said. 





  Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  February 18, 2025 12:34 ET (17:34 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Yamaha Marine Demonstrates Connected Boat® Technology with KDDI Spherience at Mobile World Congress 2025 in Barcelona, Spain
data.description:
Yamaha Marine Demonstrates Connected Boat® Technology with KDDI Spherience at Mobile World Congress 2025 in Barcelona, Spain

Yamaha Marine Demonstrates Connected Boat® Technology with KDDI Spherience at Mobile World Congress 2025 in Barcelona, Spain

PR Newswire

PLANO, Texas, Feb. 18, 2025

PLANO, Texas, Feb. 18, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- In two weeks, at Mobile World Congress in Barcelona, Spain, Yamaha Motor Corporation, U.S.A.'s Marine Business Unit and KDDI Spherience, the KDDI group's US-based connectivity Center of Excellence, will demonstrate their collaboration that will define the boating industry's software defined future.

Connected Boat® technology, Yamaha's next generation boating initiative, delivered by Yamaha Marine Systems Company, Inc's subsidiary, Siren Marine, together with KDDI and its ecosystem of partners is the next big growth opportunity in Software Defined Vehicles.

To satisfy the needs of tomorrow's customers, Yamaha believes that the connected experience is at the very center of the boating experience. The ability to monitor, track, control and maintain your boat via connected technology is a big growth opportunity for Yamaha with far reaching impacts across boat builders, Yamaha dealers and consumers.

"We intend to connect every marine product before the year 2030 as it becomes a standard part of the boating experience," says Andrew Cullen Director of Connectivity at Yamaha's US Marine Business Unit. "Partnering with KDDI and their global approach to connectivity with the user experience center stage has really helped bring our vision of Connected Boat® technology to life."

"This is the next chapter of growth in the boating industry," continues Björn Qvarsell, Head of Product Portfolio at KDDI Spherience. "The journey with Yamaha has been nothing short of amazing where it seems like not a day passes without us finding even more ways of building value for the Customer and for the benefit of our two companies and partners."

The two companies are on site in Barcelona for #MWC25 that is taking place on March 3-7, and are delivering a keynote on the Connected Industries stage, Wednesday March 5, as part of the Smart Mobility Summit. The keynote is supported by a joint partner exhibit at the GSMA Pavilion. Please visit the pavilion and the main KDDI stand #4E20, next door in Hall 4.

More information can be found at SirenMarine.com, YamahaOutboards.com and Spherience.io

SOURCE KDDI Spherience

data.title: ALSANA APPOINTS KEESHA AMEZCUA, LMFT, CEDS-C AS NEW CHIEF CLINICAL OFFICER
data.description:
ALSANA APPOINTS KEESHA AMEZCUA, LMFT, CEDS-C AS NEW CHIEF CLINICAL OFFICER

ALSANA APPOINTS KEESHA AMEZCUA, LMFT, CEDS-C AS NEW CHIEF CLINICAL OFFICER

PR Newswire

WESTLAKE VILLAGE, Calif., Feb. 18, 2025

An internationally recognized industry leader brings 20 of experience to Alsana's eating recovery community.

WESTLAKE VILLAGE, Calif., Feb. 18, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Alsana, an eating recovery community with in-person and virtual treatment programs, has appointed Keesha Amezcua, MA, LMFT, CEDS-C as its new chief clinical officer. With 20 years of eating disorder treatment experience, Amezcua brings a wealth of clinical expertise to the organization. In addition to her clinical work, she has contributed to books, articles and media on eating disorder recovery and is a frequent speaker at national conferences. She is speaking at this year's International Association of Eating Disorder Professionals' National Conference on ADHD and eating disorders entitled "ADHD, It Takes a Village: A Panel Discussion."  

Amezcua will lead all aspects of Alsana's clinical care, client experience, treatment outcomes, Adaptive Care Model®, program development, and training for clinical teams.

"Keesha's expertise and proven ability to lead treatment teams to deliver excellent care highlights our company's commitment to clinical outcomes and client experience," said Jordan Watson, Alsana's CEO. "The future of the eating disorder treatment field will be determined by those who are willing to invest in clinical leadership and work to attract, develop and train treatment teams. I am confident that under Keesha's leadership, Alsana will deliver industry-leading care, create a magnetic culture for professionals seeking to work in eating disorder treatment, and shape the future of our industry for the better."

Amezcua brings extensive clinical leadership experience over all levels of care for both adolescents and adults previously serving as Vice President of Clinical Programming for Monte Nido & Affiliates. Amezcua, in her previous role, oversaw care delivery, clinical outcomes and staff training nationally during periods of high growth. In addition, she has international program development experience contributing to Wandi Nerida, Australia's first residential eating disorder treatment center.

"I am honored to step into the role of chief clinical officer at Alsana. As we navigate the ever-changing clinical landscape, my priority is to uphold and enhance the highest standards of care for our clients while empowering, training and supporting our clinical teams," said Keesha Amezcua, Alsana's new chief clinical officer. "I have seen Alsana's commitment to evolving services that meet the needs of the moment. I am excited to contribute to this ongoing effort— driving innovation, improving client outcomes and investing in our clinical team members."

Amezcua is a Certified Eating Disorder Specialist Consultant (CEDS-C), a Licensed Marriage and Family Therapist in California, Texas and Oregon, and a Certified Daring Way Facilitator (CDWF). She has specialized training in Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) and Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT). Prior to entering the mental health field, she graduated with honors from Texas A&M University with a bachelor's degree in journalism and worked in magazine editorial, television production, and marketing and advertising.

About Alsana® 
Alsana is an eating recovery community with Virtual Partial Hospitalization (PHP) and Intensive Outpatient (IOP) programs across the United States for adults and adolescents, in addition to in-person Residential and PHP/IOP programs in Alabama and California for adults. Its eating disorder treatment programs are compassion-focused, evidence-based, and designed in alignment with Alsana's Adaptive Care Model®. This whole-person approach addresses healing in all areas of clients' lives by integrating medical, nutritional, and therapeutic care with movement and relational therapies. Alsana serves adult clients of all genders and sexual identities struggling with a broad spectrum of eating, feeding, and co-occurring mental health conditions. Alsana's programs accommodate the unique needs of vegan clients and clients struggling with ED-DMT1, also known as "diabulimia." Learn more at www.alsana.com.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/alsana-appoints-keesha-amezcua-lmft-ceds-c-as-new-chief-clinical-officer-302379192.html

SOURCE Alsana

data.title: Medtronic's stock drops on inventory hiccup and revenue miss
data.description:
By Steve Gelsi

Medtronic's adjusted third-quarter profit tops analyst estimates but its surgical-products revenue dips on temporary inventory issue

Medtronic PLC's stock fell 7.3% as the worst performer in the S&P 500 on Tuesday after the medical-products company's earnings fell slightly short of Wall Street's revenue target despite a third-quarter profit that beat estimates.

Medtronic (MDT) said third-quarter revenue fell 0.4% in its medical-surgical unit, one of the medical-products company's four main business lines, along with neuroscience, cardiovascular and diabetes.

"We experienced a change in U.S. distributor buying patterns, which had a couple of hundred-basis-point impact on our surgical performance," Chief Executive Geoff Martha said on a conference call with analysts. "We expect this to resolve as we start fiscal 2026."

Jeff Jonas, portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said he's surprised at the drop in Medtronic's stock on the heels of its "tiny miss" on organic revenue growth.

"I don't see the inventory issue as a big deal," Jonas said in an email to MarketWatch. "Yes, they should have managed it better to not let it happen, but again they're offsetting that in the current fiscal quarter ... and it won't recur next year."

Overall, Medtronic's total revenue grew nearly 3% on strength in its cutting-edge heart-surgery devices as the medical-technology company's adjusted profit topped analyst estimates.

Medtronic said its devices for a heart treatment called pulsed-field ablation (PFA) helped drive growth as it reported adjusted third-quarter earnings of $1.39 a share, ahead of the FactSet consensus estimate of $1.36 a share.

Revenue rose 2.5% to $8.29 billion, slightly below the analyst expectation of $8.33 billion.

The company said its pulsed-field-ablation product revenue increased by more than 20%.

"We are starting to see the results from our long-term investments in groundbreaking innovation, such as pulsed-field ablation," Martha said in a prepared statement.

Medtronic still expects to report adjusted earnings of $5.44 a share to $5.50 a share for 2025, compared with the analyst estimate of $5.45 a share.

The company continues to expect full-year 2025 revenue growth of 4.75% to 5% as it benefits from "the strong trajectory of our growth drivers and expected acceleration in the cardiovascular portfolio."

Medtronic also managed to increase its adjusted operating margin by 100 basis points, or 1%, to 26.2%, which was ahead of its expectations.

Pulsed-field ablation, or PFA, has been growing for its use of high-amplitude pulsed electrical fields to treat cardiovascular problems. The approach lowers the risk of stroke and other complications from the previous use of heat to treat irregular heartbeats and other conditions.

PFA is seen as a $2 billion business for Medtronic in the near future. All told, Medtronic said it's a $9 billion potential market with a growth rate in the high teens.

Including Tuesday's moves, Medtronic's stock has risen 7.7% so far in 2025, while the S&P 500 SPX is up about 4%.

-Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-18-25 1226ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: FXR Pro Fish Named Title Sponsor For 2025 Bassmaster Elite Season Opener
data.description:
FXR Pro Fish Named Title Sponsor For 2025 Bassmaster Elite Season Opener

The 2025 FXR Pro Fish Team Bassmaster Elite Will Hit The Water At St. Johns River

FXR Pro Fish, a brand driven by bringing innovation to the water, is proud to announce its partnership with the Bassmaster Elite series as the title sponsor for the first round of the new season. The 2025 FXR Pro Fish Bassmaster Elite kicks off at St. Johns River in Palatka, FL on February 20-23, 2025. The event will also be broadcast live on Fox Sports 1 and Fox Sports App, as well as the Bassmaster livestream on Bassmaster.com and YouTubeTV.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250218621439/en/

Trey McKinney FXR Pro Fish Angler (Photo: Business Wire)

"Our partnership with the Bassmaster organization as the title sponsor for the first round of the series exemplifies our dedication to the sport,” said Brian Danielson, Chief Executive Officer of FXR Pro Fish and FXR Racing. “It also comes at a perfect time for the FXR Pro Fish brand, as it follows the launch of our Spring 2025 lineup. We are excited to see our Elite roster take to the water in their fresh gear.”

The full roster of FXR Pro Fish Elite anglers includes Trey McKinney, Cooper Galant, Justin Hammer, Bryan New, Logan Parks, Matt Roberston, Bryan Schmitt, JT Thompkins, Tyler Williams, and rookie Beau Browning. The FXR Pro Fish Elite anglers will set out on the water to take down the 93-pound-6-ounces record of 2024 and kick the season off with a victory.

“Our lineup of anglers is incredibly stacked, so we are excited to watch these guys hit the water,” said Chris Meyeraan, Brand Director of FXR Pro Fish. “We may be a newer brand to the fishing world, but we are here to make our mark on the sport.”

The FXR Pro Fish Anglers will be back in action at round two of the Bassmaster Elite series at Lake Okeechobee in Okeechobee, FL from February 27 - March 2, 2025. To learn more about the FXR Pro Fish apparel or the Bassmaster series, please visit: https://fxrracing.com/pages/pro-fish or https://www.bassmaster.com/.

MEDIA CONTACT:
Neil Storz
(805)794-5352
neil@storzconsult.com

data.title: DOGE Fires FDA Employees Reviewing Musk's Neuralink, Reuters Reports, Citing Sources
data.description:
--Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency over the weekend fired U.S. Food and Drug Administration employees reviewing Musk's brain-implant company Neuralink as part of a broader plan to cut the size of the federal workforce, Reuters reports, citing two people familiar with the matter.

--About 20 people in the FDA's office of neurological and physical medicine devices were affected by the cuts, Reuters reports, citing sources. The loss of these employees will hamper the FDA's ability to quickly and safely process medical device applications of all sorts, including Neuralink's, Reuters reports, citing sources and outside experts. 




  Full article at https://www.reuters.com/world/us/fda-staff-reviewing-musks-neuralink-were-included-doge-employee-firings-sources-2025-02-17/ 





  Write to (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix) 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: SUAVE BRANDS COMPANY UNLEASHES ITS BIGGEST PRODUCT DROP IN DECADES — 34 NEW INNOVATIONS ACROSS HAIR, BODY & MEN'S CARE
data.description:
SUAVE BRANDS COMPANY UNLEASHES ITS BIGGEST PRODUCT DROP IN DECADES -- 34 NEW INNOVATIONS ACROSS HAIR, BODY & MEN'S CARE

SUAVE BRANDS COMPANY UNLEASHES ITS BIGGEST PRODUCT DROP IN DECADES -- 34 NEW INNOVATIONS ACROSS HAIR, BODY & MEN'S CARE

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, Feb. 18, 2025

Newly independent Suave doubles down on innovation, bringing salon-worthy formulas, trending ingredients, and indulgent scents—all at unbeatable prices

NEW YORK, Feb. 18, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Suave Brands Company is shaking up the beauty aisle with 34 new products, marking its boldest innovation push in years. Known for delivering quality at an accessible price, Suave is bringing luxury-level performance to everyday essentials—without the hefty price tag.

 Suave Brands is shaking up the beauty aisle with 34 new product launches, marking its boldest innovation push in years

From next-gen hair bonding technology to powerful dry shampoos, upgraded men's care, and long-lasting deodorants, this launch reinforces Suave's commitment to giving consumers more for less. Available now at retailers nationwide, these new products combine cutting-edge science, premium ingredients, and trending scents—all priced between $1.99 and $5.99. With this launch, Suave cements its place as a leader in accessible beauty, proving that consumers don't need to spend big for top-tier results.

Salon Results, Drugstore Prices

"At Suave, we believe everyone deserves great beauty products—without overpaying," said Rachel Behm, Suave Brands Co. SVP of Marketing. "We're taking innovation to the next level, bringing high-performance formulas that compete with luxury brands at a fraction of the cost. With 34 new products launching now, we're making salon-level results available for under $5."

What's New?
(product fact sheets are linked below)

WOMEN'S HAIR CARE 

  • Bonding Infusion Collection: The Bonding Infusion Collection, which includes shampoo, conditioner, purple shampoo, and treatment mask, delivers advanced damage repair - marking the first time this technology is available for under $5. The shampoo and treatment mask combo repairs damage as well as Olaplex.*
  • Shampoo & Conditioners: Hyaluronic Infusion deeply moisturizes dry & dehydrated hair; Bamboo & Hibiscus gives hair an instant boost of weightless volume.
  • Dry Shampoos in 3 variants deliver instant refresh, volume, and invigoration, working as well as Kérastase+.
  • New Heat Protection Spray shields strands from heat damage against temperatures up to 450°F.

MEN'S CARE

  • Three new Antiperspirant Deodorants with 48-hour odor & sweat protection.
  • Stress Relief Collection: Incorporates the all-day fresh scent of lavender and cedarwood in a 2-in-1 Shampoo/Conditioner, Body + Face Wash, and Deodorant.
  • Barber Essentials Collection: Offers simple, easy-to-use styling products for all hair types, available in Styling Cream, Pomade, and Gel for pro-level grooming.

WOMEN'S BODY CARE

  • Reformulated Antiperspirant Deodorants with 20% more sweat-stopping power and up to 48 hours of protection. Suave also has aluminum-free options.
  • Strawberry Delight Body Wash features the uplifting and indulging notes of strawberry, caramel, and Chantilly cream, formulated with glycerin & Vitamin E for ultra-soft skin.

KIDS

  • Limited-Edition Minions Collection: Collectible designs in celebration of the wildly popular franchise. 3-in-1, Slime Body Wash, and Detangler offer kids fun new designs of their favorite bathtime products.

*Comparisons to Olaplex and Kerastase are based on independent testing. Suave Bonding Infusion Shampoo and Treatment Mask compared to Olaplex No. 4 Bond Maintenance Shampoo and No. 5 Bond Maintenance Conditioner. In-vivo salon study measuring hair appearance, damage repair, texture and strength.

+Refreshing Dry Shampoo by Suave compared to Kérastase Fresh Affair Dry Shampoo. In-vitro testing on hair tresses measuring volume & build-up removal.

About Suave Brands Company 
Suave Brands Company was established in 2023 after Yellow Wood Partners acquired the Suave brand from Unilever, transforming it into a standalone company. The Suave Brands Co. mission is to make trusted high-quality beauty and personal care products accessible to all.

For nearly 90 years, Suave has served consumers with exceptional products at a great value. Originally founded with a vision to democratize salon-quality care, the company has evolved to offer a comprehensive range of hair and body care products serving women, men and kids. Suave has been recognized as one of TIME's World's Best Brands and Newsweek's Most Trusted Brands. Suave products can be purchased at more than 60,000 retailers across the U.S., including Walmart, Dollar General, Target, CVS, Walgreens, Family Dollar, Kroger, and Amazon. For more information, please visit www.suave.com.

CONTACT:
Holly Thomas, holly.thomas@chameleon.co

SOURCE Suave Brands Co

data.title: Videogame giants are spending more money but losing players - and profits
data.description:
By Jurica Dujmovic

Sony, Electronic Arts and Ubisoft reflect an industrywide misunderstanding (or perhaps disregard) of what gamers value

Players are increasingly sensitive to and irritated by videogames that feel forced or inauthentic.

In recent years, the videogame industry has witnessed a significant shift in the relationship between major publishers and their core audience. Giants such as Ubisoft Entertainment (FR: UBI), Sony Group (SONY) (JP: 6758) (SONY), and Electronic Arts (EA) (EA) have become increasingly disconnected from the very players they claim to entertain. This divide stems from myriad factors, including changing gamer preferences, questionable business strategies and controversial creative decisions.

Repetitive gameplay and uninspired mechanics of tired formulas have left players fatigued and craving innovative and authentic experiences - the shift to which many major publishers are still struggling to adapt. One notable trend is the waning popularity of "live service" games - titles designed for endless play and continuous monetization. "Live service" games were once hailed as the future of gaming. However, players are increasingly turning away from games that demand constant time investment without offering meaningful progression or engagement.

On the other hand, games such as "Elden Ring" and "God of War: Ragnarok," which offer rich, single-player experiences that prioritize quality over endless looping gameplay are thriving.

As major publishers struggle, indie developers have seen a surge in popularity. Games such as "Hades," "Celeste" and "Hollow Knight" have resonated strongly with players, demonstrating that tightly focused, authentic experiences can often outshine big-budget releases.

Ubisoft: A study in disconnect

Perhaps no other company better exemplifies the challenges facing major publishers than Ubisoft. Once a darling of the industry, the French videogame publisher has found itself in increasingly troubled waters.

Ubisoft's financial woes are closely tied to a series of creative decisions that have alienated its core audience. One such example is "Star Wars Outlaws," which has significantly underperformed expectations. A report by Insider Gaming paints an even grimmer picture - according to the report, "Star Wars Outlaws" managed to sell just 1 million copies in the month after its launch late last August.

This disappointing performance is particularly concerning given the game's massive budget, with Ubisoft reportedly investing around $300 million into its development. Abysmal sales figures can be attributed to several factors, including a multitude of creative restrictions that resulted in a world that felt sanitized, censored and less engaging compared with other open-world experiences.

Another example is "XDefiant," Ubisoft's free-to-play shooter game. Despite a strong launch with more than 11 million unique players, Ubisoft announced late last year that it would shut down "XDefiant," highlighting the challenges for new entries in the saturated shooter-game market. The game faced criticism for lacking a strong identity and failing to offer groundbreaking features that could pull players away from established titles.

It's worth noting that not all of Ubisoft's recent ventures were failures. "Assassin's Creed Mirage," a return to the series' roots, managed to turn a profit, generating $250 million in revenue. This success suggests that there's still a strong market for well-crafted, focused experiences that respect the franchise's core identity.

Despite this glimmer of hope, Ubisoft's future strategy raises eyebrows among industry observers. The company plans to release 10 "Assassin's Creed" titles in the next five years, a decision that has been met with skepticism. Critics argue that this aggressive release schedule is unlikely to resolve the company's core issues and may exacerbate franchise fatigue and disconnect from their audience. Ubisoft's stock price took another hit recently after the company said that its much-anticipated "Assassin's Creed Shadows" offering was being delayed for a second time; it's now slated to debut in March.

Industry struggles

To survive and thrive, publishers will need to rebuild trust.

Ubisoft's struggles are not unique. Both EA and Sony have faced similar criticism and financial consequences when their games failed to meet players' expectations. EA's "Battlefield 2042" was lambasted for lacking core features and introducing unwanted hero-based systems, resulting in a 90% player drop within months and significant revenue losses. "Anthem," another EA title, failed due to shallow, unfinished content and rigid adherence to its live-service model.

Sony blowing $400 million on its failed "Concord" offering exemplifies the dangers of giving priority to stale mechanics, lackluster gameplay and political correctness over innovation and player engagement. Its development reportedly was marred by a "design by committee" approach that stifled creativity and forced progressive representation, leading to characters who lacked relatability, depth and originality. The focus on politics over player preferences, combined with lackluster and uninspired gameplay, resulted in "Concord" being pulled from sales shortly after release.

This industrywide trend points to a growing fundamental misunderstanding (or perhaps disregard) of what current-era gamers value. Players are increasingly sensitive to and irritated by narratives that feel forced or inauthentic. They also dislike worn and tired game mechanics and seek games that organically integrate story elements in meaningful and authentic ways, rather than check off inclusivity boxes. Gamers crave experiences that break the mold in new and inspiring ways, rather than tired cookie-cutter gameplay loops, devoid of fun and creativity.

To justify their missteps and uncreative decisions, major publishers often claim they're making games that speak to contemporary audience and reflect their sensibilities. Sales figures tell a different story. Their "modern demographics" appear to be little more than a vocal minority, whose influence in social-media discourse far outweighs their actual market impact. The discrepancy between perceived demand and actual market reception provides a clear explanation for the current state of the gaming industry, where traditional audiences continue to drive the bulk of revenue despite being increasingly alienated by attempts to appeal to a niche and more progressive player base.

The path forward for major publishers is clear: To survive and thrive, publishers will need to rebuild trust by embracing the values and identities that resonate with their player base. This means moving away from imposed narratives and forced political correctness in favor of authentic storytelling and gameplay that respect players' intelligence. Companies should focus on creating protagonists and stories that gamers can genuinely connect with.

To achieve this, publishers must engage in more open and honest communication with their communities through transparent development processes, clear explanations of creative decisions, and a genuine willingness to incorporate player feedback. Instead of trying to proselytize their audience, companies should listen to and learn from the gamers who have supported their franchises for years.
